8660.text: Where the applicant is an enforcement officer , he shall not provide such evidence as is referred to in Rule 51.15 unless directed by the Court to do so.

8662.text: Any person who makes a claim under Rule 51.3 and who is served with a claim form under Rule 51.14 shall within 14 days serve on the execution creditor and the sheriff an affidavit specifying and/or describing any assets claimed and setting out the grounds upon which such claim is based.

8664.text: Where the applicant is an enforcement officer a claim form under Rule 51.13 must give notice of the requirement in Rule 51.17.

8665.text: Powers Of Court Hearing The Claim

8667.text: Where on the hearing of a claim under this Part all the persons by whom adverse claims to the subjectmatter in dispute (hereafter in this Part referred to as ‘the interpleader claimants’) appear, the Court may order:

8668.text: (1) that any interpleader claimant be made a defendant in any claim pending with respect to the subject-matter in dispute in substitution for or in addition to the applicant for relief under this Part; or

8669.text: (2) that an issue between the interpleader claimants be stated and tried and may direct which of the interpleader claimants is to be claimants and which defendant .

8672.text: (1) the applicant under this Part is an enforcement officer ;

8673.text: (2) all the interpleader claimants consent or any of them so requests; or

8674.text: (3) the question at issue between the interpleader claimants is a question of law and the facts are not in dispute;

8675.text: the Court may immediately determine the question at issue between the interpleader claimants and make an order accordingly on such terms as may be just.

8677.text: Where an interpleader claimant, having been duly served with a claim form under this Part, does not appear at the hearing or, having appeared, fails or refuses to comply with an order made in the proceedings, the Court may make an order declaring the interpleader claimant, and all persons claiming under him, barred from prosecuting his claim against the applicant for such relief and all persons claiming under him, but such an order shall not affect the rights of the interpleader claimants as between

8679.text: Power To Order Sale Of Assets Taken In Execution

8681.text: Where an application for relief under this Part is made by an enforcement officer who has taken possession of any assets in execution under any process, and an interpleader claimant alleges that he is entitled to the assets by way of security for debt, the Court may order those assets or any part thereof to be sold and may direct that the proceeds of sale be applied in such manner and on such terms as may be just and as may be specified in the order.

8684.text: Where a defendant to a claim applies for relief under this Part in the claim, the Court may by order stay all further proceedings in the claim.

8687.text: The Court may in or for the purposes of any interpleader proceedings make such order as to costs or any other matter as it thinks just.

8689.text: Where the interpleader claimant fails to appear at the hearing, the Court may direct that the enforcement officer’s and execution creditor’s costs shall be assessed by the Registrar .

8692.text: Where the Court considers it necessary or expedient to make an order in any interpleader proceedings in several proceedings, the Court may make such an order; and the order shall bear the titles of all those proceedings and shall be binding on all the parties to them.

8695.text: Parts 19 and 28 shall, with the necessary modifications, apply in relation to an interpleader issue as they apply in relation to any other proceedings.

8698.text: Part 35 shall, with the necessary modifications, apply to the trial of an interpleader issue as it applies to the trial of a claim.

8700.text: The Court may give such judgment or make such order as is necessary finally to dispose of all questions arising in the interpleader proceedings.

8704.text:
52.1   
The power of the Court of First Instance or Court of Appeal to punish for contempt of Court may be exercised by an order of committal.

8706.text: An order of committal may be made by a single Judge of the Court .

8708.text:
52.3   
An application for an order of committal must be made by claim form or application notice and be supported by an affidavit .

8709.text: 52.4 A committal application must, subject to Rule 52.5, be commenced by the issue of a Part 8 claim form.

8710.text:
52.5   
If the committal application is made in existing proceedings it must be commenced by the filing of an application notice in those proceedings.

8712.text: An application to commit for breach of an undertaking or order must be commenced by the filing of an

8713.text: application notice in the proceedings in which the undertaking was given or the order was made.

8715.text: The application notice must state that the application is made in the proceedings in question and its title and reference number must correspond with the title and reference number of those proceedings.

8717.text: If the committal application is commenced by the issue of a claim form, Part 8 shall, subject to the provisions of this Part, apply as though references to ‘claimant’ were references to the person making the committal application and references to ‘defendant’ were references to the person against whom the committal application is made (in this practice direction referred to as ‘the respondent’) but:

8718.text: (1) the claim form together with copies of all written evidence in support must, unless the Court otherwise directs, be served personally on the respondent;

8719.text: (2) the claim form must set out in full the grounds on which the committal application is made and must identify, separately and numerically, each alleged act of contempt including, if known, the date of each alleged act;

8720.text: (3) an amendment to the claim form can be made with the permission of the Court but not otherwise;

8722.text: (5) the claim form must contain a prominent notice stating the possible consequences of the Court making a committal order and of the respondent not attending the hearing. A form of notice, which may be used, is annexed to this Part at Schedule A.

8724.text: If a committal application is commenced by the filing of an application notice , Part 23 shall, subject to the provisions of this Part, apply, but:

8725.text: (1) the application notice together with copies of all written evidence in support must, unless the Court otherwise directs, be served personally on the respondent;

8726.text: (2) the application notice must set out in full the grounds on which the committal application is made and must identify, separately and numerically, each alleged act of contempt including, if known, the date of each of the alleged acts;

8727.text: (3) an amendment to the application notice can be made with the permission of the Court but not otherwise;

8728.text: (4) the Court may not dispose of the committal application without a hearing; and

8729.text: (5) the application notice must contain a prominent notice stating the possible consequences of the Court making a committal order and of the respondent not attending the hearing. A form of notice, which may be used, is annexed to this Part at Schedule A.

8731.text: Without prejudice to its powers under Part 9, the Court may dispense with service under Rule 52.8(1) or Rule 52.9(1) if it thinks it just to do so.

8732.text: Saving For Power To Commit Without Application

8734.text: Nothing in this Part shall be taken as affecting the power of the Court of First Instance or Court of Appeal to make an order of committal on its own initiative against a person guilty of contempt of Court .

8737.text: Written evidence in support of or in opposition to a committal application must be given by affidavit .

8739.text: Written evidence served in support of or in opposition to a committal application must, unless the Court otherwise directs, be filed.

8741.text: A respondent may give oral evidence at the hearing, whether or not he has filed or served any written evidence. If he does so, he may be cross-examined.

8743.text: A respondent may, with the permission of the Court , call a witness to give oral evidence at the hearing whether or not the witness has sworn an affidavit .

8746.text: The applicant for the committal order must, when lodging the claim form or application notice with the Court for issuing or filing , as the case may be, obtain from the Court a date for the hearing of the committal application.

8748.text: Unless the Court otherwise directs, the hearing date of a committal application shall be not less than 14 days after service of the claim form or of the application notice , as the case may be, on the respondent. The hearing date must be specified in the claim form or application notice or in a Notice of Hearing or Application attached to and served with the claim form or application notice .

8750.text: The Court may, however, at any time give case management directions, including directions for the service

8751.text: of written evidence by the respondent and written evidence in reply by the applicant, or may hold a directions hearing.

8753.text: The Court may on the hearing date:

8754.text: (1) give case management directions with a view to a hearing of the committal application on a future date; or

8755.text: (2) if the committal application is ready to be heard, proceed to hear it.

8757.text: In dealing with any committal application, the Court will have regard to the need for the respondent to have details of the alleged acts of contempt and the opportunity to respond to the committal application.

8759.text: The Court should also have regard to the need for the respondent to be:

8760.text: (1) allowed a reasonable time for responding to the committal application including, if necessary, preparing a defence;

8761.text: (2) given the opportunity, if unrepresented, to obtain legal advice; and

8762.text: (3) if unable to understand English, allowed to make arrangements, seeking the assistance of the Court if necessary, for an interpreter to attend the hearing.

8765.text: The Court may, on application by the respondent or on its own initiative, strike out a committal application if it appears to the Court:

8766.text: (1) that the committal application and the evidence served in support of it disclose no reasonable ground for alleging that the respondent is guilty of a contempt of Court ;

8767.text: (2) that the committal application is an abuse of the Court’s process or, if made in existing proceedings, is otherwise likely to obstruct the just disposal of those proceedings; or

8768.text: (3) that there has been a failure to comply with a Rule, Practice Direction or Court order.

8771.text: Rules 31.29 to 31.49 do not apply to committal applications.

8773.text: An order under Rule 19.1 may not be made against a respondent to a committal application.

8775.text: A committal application may not be discontinued without the permission of the Court .

8777.text: The Court may waive any procedural defect in the commencement or conduct of a committal application if satisfied that no injustice has been caused to the respondent by the defect.

8780.text: A committal application should normally be heard in public.

8782.text: The Court hearing an application for an order of committal may sit in private in the following cases, that is to say:

8785.text: If the Court hearing an application in private by virtue of Rule 52.28 decides to make an order of committal against the person sought to be committed, it shall state in public:

8786.text:
(1) the name of the respondent;   
(2) in general terms the nature of the contempt or contempts found proved; and (3) the penalty (if any) imposed.

8788.text: Except with the permission of the Court hearing an application for an order of committal, no grounds shall be relied upon at the hearing except the grounds set out in the claim form or application notice .

8790.text: If on the hearing of the application the person sought to be committed expresses a wish to give oral

8791.text: evidence on his own behalf, he shall be entitled to do so.

8792.text: Power To Suspend Execution Of Committal Order

8794.text: The Court may by order direct that the execution of the order of committal shall be suspended for such period or on such terms or conditions as it may specify.

8796.text: Where execution of an order of committal is suspended by an order under Rule 52.32, the applicant for the order of committal must, unless the Court otherwise directs, serve on the person against whom it was made a notice informing him of the making and terms of the order under that Rule.

8797.text: Contempt In The Face Of The Court

8799.text: Where the committal application relates to a contempt in the face of the Court the following matters should be given particular attention. Normally, it will be appropriate to defer consideration of the behaviour to allow the respondent time to reflect on what has occurred. The time needed for the following procedures should allow for such a period of reflection.

8801.text: A Part 8 claim form and an application notice are not required for committal for contempt in the face of the Court , but other provisions of this Part should be applied, as necessary, or adapted to the circumstances. In addition the Judge should:

8803.text: (5) allow the respondent an opportunity to:

8805.text: (b) explain his actions and behaviour; and

8806.text: (c) if the contempt is proved, address the Court on the penalty to be imposed on him;

8807.text: (6) if there is a risk of the appearance of bias, ask another Judge to hear the committal application; and (7) where appropriate, nominate a suitable person to give the respondent the information.

8808.text: (It is likely to be appropriate to nominate a person where the effective communication of information by the Judge to the respondent was not possible when the incident occurred).

8810.text: Where the committal application is to be heard by another Judge , a written statement by the Judge before whom the actions and behaviour of the respondent which have given rise to the committal application took place may be submitted as evidence of those actions and behaviour.

8813.text: Nothing in this Part shall be taken as affecting the power of the Court:

8814.text: (1) to make an order referring the matter to the Attorney General of Dubai; or

8815.text: (2) to make any other order it considers necessary in the interests of justice (including the giving security for his good behaviour), pursuant to Article 43 of the Court Law 2004; or

8816.text: (3) to make an order requiring a person guilty of contempt of Court , or a person punishable by virtue of any enactment in like manner as if he had been guilty of contempt of Court , to pay a fine or to give security for his good behaviour;

8817.text: and the provisions of this Part, so far as applicable, and with the necessary modifications, shall apply in relation to an application for such an order as they apply in relation to an application for an order of committal.

8823.text: (1) special procedure for dealing with claims (“small claims”) which are issued in or have been transferred to the Small Claims Tribunal (“the SCT”); and

8824.text: (2) limits the amount of costs that can be recovered in respect of a small claim.

8826.text: The SCT will hear and determine claims within the jurisdiction of the DIFC Courts:

8827.text: (1) where the amount of the claim or the value of the subject matter of the claim does not exceed AED 500,000 or;

8828.text: (2) where the claim relates to the employment or former employment of a party; and

8829.text: all parties elect in writing that it be heard by the SCT (there is no value limit for the SCT’s elective jurisdiction in the context of employment claims); or

8830.text: (3) which do not fall within the provisions of sub-paragraphs (1) or (2) above, but in respect of which:

8831.text: (a) the amount of the claim or the value of the subject matter of the claim does not exceed AED 1,000,000; and

8832.text: (b) all parties to the claim elect in writing that it be heard by the SCT, and such election is made in the underlying contract (if any) or subsequent to execution of that contract

8833.text: (4) such other claims as may be ordered or directed by the Chief Justice to be heard by the SCT from time to time.

8835.text: (1) Where parties have elected the SCT’s jurisdiction in writing, neither party shall be able to withdraw such election without the approval of an SCT Judge.

8836.text: (2) Where a claimant issues multiple claims against the same defendant, the SCT Judge may, where appropriate treat those claims as a single consolidated claim under Rule 4.2(7) for the purposes of deciding whether the amount in dispute exceeds the limits of the SCT’s jurisdiction.

8838.text: Reference in this Part to “claims” shall include reference to counterclaims, save that, where a counterclaim would not otherwise be within the SCT’s jurisdiction, an SCT Judge may direct that the proceedings be transferred to the Court of First Instance

8841.text: References in this Part to filing a document are references to filing that document with the SCT Registrar, unless stated otherwise.

8842.text: SCT Judge’s power to grant a final remedy

8844.text: The SCT Judge may grant any final remedy in relation to a small claim which a Judge of the Court of First Instance of the DIFC Courts could grant if the proceedings were before that Court.

8845.text: Extent To Which Other Parts Apply

8847.text: The following Parts of the RDC shall apply to small claims except to the extent that a Rule limits such application or the SCT Judge orders otherwise:

8850.text: The SCT of its own initiative may order a party to provide further information if it considers it appropriate to do so.

8853.text: A small claim must be started by a Claim Form using Form P53/01.

8855.text: A small claim is started when the SCT Registrar issues a Claim Form at the request of a party.

8857.text: The claimant must set out in or attach to the Claim Form a statement summarising the remedy sought and the claimant’s reasons for claiming that he is entitled to that remedy. The claimant must include on the face of the Claim Form a statement of the monetary value of the small claim.

8859.text: The claimant must include in the Claim Form the name and address for service of the defendant, together with any other available contact information. Where the defendant is a company, the claimant shall state the address where, to the best of his knowledge, the company carries on its business.

8861.text: The SCT will serve the claim form on defendant, unless otherwise directed by the Registry.

8864.text: Within 7 days after he is served with a claim form in respect of a matter which has been referred to the SCT, a defendant must:

8865.text: (1) Admit the claim by filing and serving on the claimant an admission in accordance with RDC Part 15;

8866.text: (2) File a defence to the claim setting out (a) which parts of the claim are admitted; (b) which parts are denied and his reasons for denying those parts; and (c) the details of any counterclaim; or

8867.text: (3) Make an application to dispute the jurisdiction of the SCT, supported by evidence.

8869.text: Where the defendant admits the claim in accordance with Rule 53.14(1), the SCT shall issue an order giving judgment on the claim.

8871.text: Where the defendant files and serves an application to dispute the jurisdiction in accordance with Rule 53.14(3), the application will be heard by an SCT Judge in accordance with RDC Part 23.”

8874.text: After the defendant has filed and served a defence in accordance with Rule 53.14(2) and/or after the time for filing such a statement has passed, the SCT will fix a time for the parties to attend before an SCT Judge for a consultation.

8876.text: The primary purpose of the consultation will be to allow the parties to attempt to resolve their dispute.

8878.text: The SCT may treat the consultation as the final hearing of the claim if all the parties agree.

8880.text:
The SCT will generally fix the consultation within 7 days after the time for filing a defence has expired (i.e.   
within 14 days after service of the claim form).

8882.text: When filing the Claim Formthe claimant should inform the SCT of any dates within that period on which he is unable to attend the consultation and his reasons for being unable to attend.

8884.text: When filing a defence, the defendant should inform the SCT of any dates within that period on which he is unable to attend the consultation and his reasons for being unable to attend.

8886.text: The SCT will attempt to fix the consultation on a date on which both parties are able to attend.

8888.text: If any party is unable to attend the consultation on the date fixed, he must notify the SCT and the other party as soon as possible and give his reasons for being unable to attend. If it appears to the SCT that the party has a good reason for being unable to attend the consultation, the SCT may adjourn the consultation and fix a new date.

8890.text: It is essential that each party attends the consultation in person. Parties should not be represented by a lawyer. A party may be represented by a non-lawyer where it appears to the SCT that it is reasonably necessary.

8892.text: Any of its full-time officers or employees may represent a corporate party at the consultation.

8894.text: Each party should file with his claim form or defence any documents on which he wishes to rely at the consultation.

8896.text: If a party fails to attend the consultation, the SCT Judge may:

8897.text: (1) decide the small claim against that party; or (2) adjourn the consultation.

8899.text: If neither party attends the consultation, the SCT Judge may:

8900.text: (1) dismiss the claim; or (2) adjourn the consultation.

8902.text: A party who was neither present nor represented at the consultation and against whom the claim has been decided in accordance with Rule 53.28 or Rule 53.29 may apply for that order to be set aside and the claim reinstated.

8904.text: A party who applies for an Order to be set aside in accordance with Rule 53.30, must make the application not more than 7 days after the day on which notice of the Order was served on him.

8906.text: The SCT may grant an application under Rule 53.30 only if the applicant:

8907.text: (1) had a good reason for not attending the consultation; and (2) has a real prospect of success in the small claim.

8909.text: If the SCT grants an application to set aside an Order under Rule 53.30:

8910.text:
(1) the SCT will fix a new date for the consultation; and   
(2) the consultation may take place immediately after the hearing of the application to set aside the Order and may be dealt with by the SCT Judge who set aside the Order.

8912.text: Unless the SCT Judge orders otherwise, the consultation shall take place in private.

8914.text: If the claim is settled at the consultation, the SCT Judge conducting the consultation will issue a consent order, recording the terms of the settlement.

8916.text: If the claim is not settled at the consultation, the SCT Judge may either:

8917.text: (1) fix a date for a further consultation; or

8918.text: (2) make arrangements for the hearing of the claim in accordance with RDC Rules 53.38 to 53.42 below.

8921.text: Where appropriate, the SCT Judge may order that the small claim be transferred to the Court of First Instance. When deciding whether to do so, the SCT Judge shall have regard to the following matters:

8925.text: If the claim is not settled at the consultation, unless he fixes a further consultation or Rule 53.37 applies, the SCT Judge will give directions for the preparation of the small claim for trial.

8927.text: The SCT Judge at the consultation will generally:

8930.text: A party may ask the SCT Judge at the consultation to give particular directions about the conduct of the case.

8932.text: In deciding whether to make an order for exchange of witness statements the SCT Judge will have regard to the following:

8935.text: No expert may give evidence, whether written or oral, at a hearing without the permission of the SCT.

8936.text: Power Of The Sct To Add To, Vary Or Revoke Directions

8938.text: The SCT may add to, vary or revoke directions.

8941.text: Unless the parties agree otherwise, the hearing of the claim will not be conducted by the same SCT Judge who conducted the consultation.

8943.text: The SCT Judge may adopt any method of proceeding at a hearing that he considers to be fair.

8947.text: The general rule is that a small claim hearing will be in private, unless the parties agree or the SCT Judge orders otherwise.

8949.text: The strict rules of evidence do not apply.

8951.text: The SCT Judge may take evidence on oath but is not required to do so.

8953.text: The SCT Judge may limit cross-examination.

8955.text: The SCT Judge may in particular:

8956.text: (1) ask questions of all or any of the witnesses himself before allowing any other person to ask questions of any witnesses;

8957.text: (2) refuse to allow cross-examination of any witness until all the witnesses have given evidence in chief; and/or

8958.text: (3) limit cross-examination of a witness to a fixed time or to a particular subject or issue, or both.

8962.text: (1) Unless the SCT Judge orders otherwise, a party should present his own case at a hearing.

8963.text: (2) A party may be represented at the hearing by a non-lawyer or lawyer[1] only after obtaining permission from the SCT which is to be given where it appears to the SCT on reasonable grounds that it is necessary in the circumstances.

8964.text: (3) If a party is allowed to be represented by a lawyer, or in house Counsel as permitted under Rule 53.53, the opposing party shall be informed and given the opportunity to be represented at the hearing.

8965.text: (4) Parties’ attention is drawn to RDC Part 53.70.

8968.text: Any of its full-time officers or employees (including in house Counsel) may represent a corporate party.

8970.text: The SCT will take a minute of or otherwise record by such means as the SCT Judge considers appropriate any hearing that takes place at the SCT. A party may obtain a copy of that minute or other recording on payment of the proper charges specified by the SCT.

8972.text: It is not permissible for a party to use its own recording devices in the SCT.

8974.text: The SCT Judge must give reasons for his decision.

8976.text: The SCT Judge may give reasons for his judgment as briefly and simply as the nature of the case requires.

8978.text: He will normally do so orally at the hearing, but he may give them later at a hearing either orally or in writing.

8980.text: Where the SCT Judge decides the case without a hearing under Rule 53.64, the SCT Judge will prepare a note of his reasons and the SCT will send a copy to each party.

8981.text: Non-attendance of parties at a final hearing

8983.text: If a claimant does not attend the hearing, the SCT may strike out the claim.

8985.text: If a defendant does not attend the hearing and the claimant does attend the hearing, the SCT may decide the claim on the basis of the evidence of the claimant alone.

8987.text: If neither party attends the hearing, the SCT may strike out the claim and any defence and counterclaim.

8989.text: Nothing in these provisions affects the general power of the SCT to adjourn a hearing, for example where a party who wishes to attend a hearing on the date fixed cannot do so for a good reason.

8992.text: The SCT may, if all parties agree, deal with the claim without a hearing.

8995.text: A party who was not present at the hearing of the claim may apply for an order that a Judgment under this Part shall be set aside and the claim re-heard.

8997.text: A party who applies for an order setting aside a Judgment under Rule 53.65 must make the application not more than 7 days after the day on which notice of the Judgment was served on him.

8999.text: The SCT may grant an application under Rule 53.65 only if the applicant:

9000.text: (1) had a good reason for not attending the hearing; and (2) has a real prospect of success at the hearing.

9002.text: If a Judgment is set aside:

9003.text: (1) the SCT must fix a new hearing for the claim; and (2) the hearing may take place immediately after the hearing of the application to set the Judgment aside and may be dealt with by the SCT Judge who set aside the Judgment.

9005.text: A party may not apply to set aside a Judgment under this rule if the SCT dealt with the claim without a hearing under Rule 53.64.

9008.text: The SCT may not order a party to a small claim to pay a sum to another party in respect of that other party’s costs, fees and expenses, including those relating to an appeal, except:

9009.text: (1) such part of any Court or Tribunal fees paid by that other party as the SCT may consider appropriate; (2) such further costs as the SCT may assess by the summary procedure and order to be paid by a party who has behaved unreasonably.

9011.text: A party’s rejection of an offer in settlement will not of itself constitute unreasonable behaviour under Rule 53.70(2), but the SCT may take it into consideration when it is applying the unreasonableness test.

9012.text: Claim re-allocated from the SCT to the Court of First Instance

9014.text: Where a claim is transferred from the SCT to the Court of First Instance, Rule 53.70 (costs in the SCT) will cease to apply after the claim has been transferred and the Costs Rules set out in RDC Parts 38–40 will apply from the date of transfer.

9017.text: An Order of the SCT shall have the same status as an Order f the Court of First Instance of the DIFC Courts and may be enforced in accordance with the rules and procedures set out in RDC Parts 45–50.

9019.text: Applications for the enforcement of an Order of the SCT should be made to the Court of First Instance.

9024.text: Attention is drawn to FRDC 44.36 / ARDC 44.10 which specifies that, subject to any Order of the SCT, an appellant should file his appellant’s notice seeking permission to appeal with the Court of First Instance within 14 days (FRDC) or 21 days (ARDC) after the date of the decision which the appellant wishes to appeal.

9026.text: Attention is also drawn to FRDC 44.143 / ARDC 44.118 which sets out the circumstances in which the Court of First Instance will allow an appeal from a decision of a Tribunal.

9028.text: An appellant’s Notice must be filed and served in Form P53/02.

9030.text: Where the SCT dealt with the claim to which the appellant is a party under Rule 53.64 without a hearing, an application for permission to appeal must be made to the Court of First Instance.

9032.text: Where an appeal is allowed the Appeal Court will, if possible, dispose of the case at the same time without referring the claim to the lower court or ordering a new hearing. It may do so without hearing further evidence.

9034.text: Permission to appeal an SCT Judgment may be made subject to conditions when the Judge granting permission considers it appropriate to do so. Such conditions may include the costs of appeal.

9035.text: Waiver of fees or fee deferral

9037.text: Where a party seeks an order that the Court fee associated with his claim should be waived:

9038.text: (1) He must state this in his claim form;

9039.text: (2) He must set out in his claim form or attach to his claim form a statement of the grounds on which he seeks waiver of the court fees;

9040.text: (3) The SCT Registrar shall decide on paper whether to waive the fees or defer payment of the fees until the end of the case;

9041.text: (4) Where Court fees are waived, the SCT Registry shall record that fact on the case file.

9048.text: “block transfer order” has the meaning in Rule 54.228;

9049.text: “the Companies Law” means the Companies Law 2009;

9050.text: “the Court Law” means the DIFC Courts Law 2004;

9051.text: “insolvency proceedings” means any proceedings under the Law, the Regulations or this Part of these Rules ;

9053.text: “office-holder” means in relation to insolvency proceedings any person who by virtue of any provision of the Law or the Regulations holds an office in relation to those proceedings;

9054.text: “outgoing office-holder” has the meaning in Rule 54.227;

9055.text: “the petitioner” or “the petitioning creditor” includes, in winding up, any person who has been substituted as such, or has been given carriage of the petition;

9056.text: “the Regulations” means the Insolvency Regulations 2009 (and any reference to a numbered regulation is to that regulation within the Regulations);

9057.text: “replacement office-holder” has the meaning in Rule 54.227; and

9058.text: “substantive application” is that part of the application in Rule 54.230(1) and 54.230(2)

9060.text: For the purposes of this Part a “Company” includes:

9061.text: 54.2.1 a Recognised Company in respect of which a winding-up order is sought or which is being wound up by the Court pursuant to Part 6 of the Law save as otherwise provided or the context otherwise requires; and

9062.text: 54.2.2 a Foreign Company where and insofar as the context requires.

9064.text: Other terms which have a defined meaning in the Law, the Companies Law, the Regulations and these Rules have the same meaning in this Part.

9067.text: The provisions of Rules 2.11 to 2.15 (Time) apply, as regards computation of time, to anything required or authorised to be done in this Part.

9069.text: The provisions of Rule 4.2(1) (The Court’s general powers of management) apply so as to enable the Court to extend or shorten the time for compliance with anything required or authorised to be done by this Part.

9072.text: Save where these Rules provide otherwise, anything to be done under or by virtue of the Law, the Regulations or this Part of the Rules by, to or before the Court may be done by, to or before a Judge or a Registrar.

9076.text: This Section applies to any application made to the Court under the Law or the Regulations except (save as otherwise provided):

9077.text: (1) a petition for a winding-up order under Article 50 of the Law (see Section III);

9078.text: (2) an application for the appointment of a provisional liquidator (see Section IV); and
